g.data-narration: Welcome to Relational Model Concepts. This foundational topic by E.F. Codd revolutionized database management systems worldwide.
The Relational Model represents data as tables with rows and columns, providing a mathematical foundation for organizing data.
Modern DBMS like Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL rely on this model for efficient storage, retrieval, and maintaining data integrity.
g.text: Foundation for all modern database systems

g.data-narration: An entity is a real-world object uniquely identifiable, like a student. An entity set is a collection of similar entities, like all students.
g.text: Entity: Real-world object that can be uniquely identified
Collection of entities of same type

g.data-narration: Attributes are properties of entities. They can be simple, composite, single-valued, multi-valued, or derived based on their characteristics.
Keys uniquely identify tuples. Superkeys can have extra attributes. Candidate keys are minimal superkeys. Primary key is the chosen one.
g.text: Keys: Superkey → Candidate Key → Primary Key

g.data-narration: Foreign keys establish relationships between tables, referencing primary keys. Unique keys are like primary keys but allow NULL values.
Relationships associate entities. The degree indicates how many entity sets participate. Binary involves two entities, ternary involves three.
Cardinality specifies how entities relate: one-to-one, one-to-many, or many-to-many. This determines how relationships are implemented.

g.text: Each entity links to at most one other
One entity, many in other set

g.data-narration: A schema defines structure (name, attributes, domains). An instance is actual data - the tuples stored at a particular moment.
Domains define allowable values for attributes. They specify data types, ranges, and formats ensuring data consistency and integrity.
g.text: Set of allowable values for an attribute

g.data-narration: Integrity constraints ensure database accuracy: domain constraints, key constraints, entity integrity, and referential integrity must be maintained.
Entity integrity ensures primary keys uniquely identify records and cannot be NULL. Referential integrity maintains valid foreign key references.

g.text: Primary Key ≠ NULL | Must be unique
FK → PK reference must exist or be NULL

g.data-narration: For STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, Age, Branch, Email), Roll_No and Email are candidate keys; Roll_No becomes the primary key.
g.text: STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, Age, Branch, Email, Phone)

g.data-narration: For M:N relationship Sells between STORES and PRODUCTS with Quantity, create three tables: STORE, PRODUCT, and SELLS with composite key.
Remember: Keys follow Superkey → Candidate Key → Primary Key hierarchy. Entity integrity = PK not NULL. Foreign keys maintain relationships.

g.text: PK must be UNIQUE + NOT NULL
